scanner_description: Detects production configuration issues that will cause outages, data leaks, or degraded performance in deployed environments.

rules.name: NODE_ENV Hardcoded to Development
rules.note: NODE_ENV is hardcoded to 'development'. In production this enables verbose errors, disables caching, and may expose stack traces to users.
rules.remediation: Remove hardcoded value. Set NODE_ENV=production in deployment environment variables, not in code.

rules.name: Source Maps Enabled in Production Config
rules.note: Source maps expose original source code, variable names, and application logic to anyone inspecting the deployed bundle.
rules.remediation: Set devtool: false (webpack), sourcemap: false (vite), or productionSourceMap: false (vue-cli) in production builds.

rules.name: No Health Check Endpoint
rules.note: Server has no health check endpoint. Load balancers and orchestrators cannot verify the app is running, leading to routing traffic to dead instances.
rules.remediation: Add a GET /health or /healthz endpoint that returns 200 when the app is ready to serve traffic. Include DB connectivity check if applicable.

rules.name: No Graceful Shutdown Handler
rules.note: Server does not handle SIGTERM/SIGINT. Deployments will kill in-flight requests instead of draining them, causing 502 errors during deploys.
rules.remediation: Handle process.on('SIGTERM') to close the server, drain connections, and exit cleanly. Most cloud platforms send SIGTERM before SIGKILL.

rules.name: No Error Tracking Integrated
rules.note: No error tracking service detected (Sentry, Bugsnag, Rollbar, Datadog, New Relic). Production errors will go unnoticed until users report them.
rules.remediation: Integrate an error tracking service. Sentry is free for small teams: npm install @sentry/node && Sentry.init({ dsn: '...' })

rules.name: Stack Traces Sent to Client
rules.note: Error stack traces are sent in API responses. This exposes file paths, line numbers, library versions, and internal architecture to attackers.
rules.remediation: Log full errors server-side. Return generic error messages to clients: res.status(500).json({ error: 'Internal server error' })

rules.name: Raw Error Messages Sent to Client
rules.note: Raw error messages forwarded to clients may leak database column names, query syntax, or internal service details.
rules.remediation: Sanitize error messages before returning. Map known errors to user-friendly messages, default to generic for unknown.

rules.name: TLS Certificate Verification Disabled
rules.note: TLS certificate verification is disabled (rejectUnauthorized: false). This accepts self-signed and expired certs, enabling man-in-the-middle attacks.
rules.remediation: Remove rejectUnauthorized: false. If a self-signed cert is needed for dev, use NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS environment variable instead.

rules.name: NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED Set to 0
rules.note: Global TLS rejection is disabled for the entire process. Every HTTPS connection in this app is vulnerable to MITM attacks.
rules.remediation: Remove this setting entirely. Fix the underlying certificate issue instead of disabling verification globally.

rules.name: SSL Verification Disabled (Python requests)
rules.note: requests.get/post with verify=False skips TLS certificate validation. All data sent over this connection can be intercepted.
rules.remediation: Remove verify=False. If using a custom CA, pass the CA bundle path: verify='/path/to/ca-bundle.crt'

rules.name: No HTTPS Enforcement Detected
rules.note: No HTTPS redirect or enforcement detected. Users accessing via HTTP will transmit credentials and data in plaintext.
rules.remediation: Use helmet.hsts() or add middleware to redirect HTTP to HTTPS. If behind a reverse proxy, check X-Forwarded-Proto header.

rules.name: Database Connection Without SSL
rules.note: PostgreSQL connection string without sslmode=require. Database traffic is unencrypted and queryable by network observers.
rules.remediation: Add ?sslmode=require (or sslmode=verify-full for maximum security) to the connection string.

rules.name: MongoDB Connection Without TLS
rules.note: MongoDB connection string without tls=true. All queries and data transit unencrypted.
rules.remediation: Add ?tls=true to the MongoDB connection string. Use mongodb+srv:// which enables TLS by default.

rules.name: Node Started Without Process Manager
rules.note: Start script runs bare 'node' without PM2 or similar. If the process crashes, it stays down until manually restarted.
rules.remediation: Use PM2 for production: pm2 start app.js --name myapp -i max. Or ensure Docker/systemd handles restarts.

rules.name: Production Logging via console.log Only
rules.note: Using console.log for production logging. No structured logging, no log levels, no log rotation — makes debugging production issues extremely difficult.
rules.remediation: Use a structured logger like Pino or Winston: const logger = pino({ level: 'info' }). Route logs to a log aggregation service.

rules.name: Django ALLOWED_HOSTS is Empty
rules.note: Empty ALLOWED_HOSTS means Django will reject all requests when DEBUG=False, causing a complete outage.
rules.remediation: Set ALLOWED_HOSTS to your domain(s): ALLOWED_HOSTS = ['yourdomain.com', 'www.yourdomain.com']

rules.name: Gunicorn Running Single Worker
rules.note: Gunicorn configured with 1 worker. A single slow request blocks all traffic. Production should use (2×CPU)+1 workers.
rules.remediation: Set workers to (2 * num_cores) + 1. Example: gunicorn --workers 5 app:app

rules.name: Static Assets Served Without Cache Headers
rules.note: express.static() without maxAge means browsers re-download every asset on every page load, increasing load times and server costs.
rules.remediation: express.static('public', { maxAge: '1y', immutable: true }) for hashed assets, or maxAge: '1h' for non-hashed.

rules.name: No Response Compression
rules.note: Express app without compression middleware. Responses are sent uncompressed, increasing bandwidth 60-80% for text content.
rules.remediation: npm install compression && app.use(compression()). Or handle compression at the reverse proxy/CDN level.

rules.name: Vercel Config Missing Security Headers
rules.note: vercel.json defines custom headers but omits security headers (X-Frame-Options, CSP, HSTS).
rules.remediation: Add security headers to your vercel.json headers configuration.

rules.name: Netlify Config Missing Security Headers
rules.note: netlify.toml has no custom headers section. Security headers like CSP, HSTS, and X-Frame-Options are not configured.
rules.remediation: Add [[headers]] sections to netlify.toml with security header values.

rules.name: Hardcoded Port Number
rules.note: Server listens on a hardcoded port. Most cloud platforms assign a dynamic port via PORT env var — this will fail to start.
rules.remediation: Use: app.listen(process.env.PORT || 3000)

rules.name: COPY . . Without .dockerignore
rules.note: Dockerfile copies entire context. Without .dockerignore, node_modules, .env, .git, and secrets may end up in the image.
rules.remediation: Create .dockerignore with: node_modules, .git, .env, *.log, dist, coverage, .DS_Store

rules.name: No Server Request Timeout
rules.note: HTTP server has no request timeout. Slow clients or intentional slowloris attacks will hold connections open indefinitely, exhausting server resources.
rules.remediation: Set server.timeout and server.headersTimeout: server.timeout = 30000; server.headersTimeout = 31000;

rules.name: No CORS Configuration
rules.note: API server has mutation endpoints but no CORS middleware. Browsers will block cross-origin requests, or worse, a permissive default may be inherited.
rules.remediation: npm install cors && app.use(cors({ origin: 'https://yourdomain.com', credentials: true }))

rules.name: setInterval Without clearInterval
rules.note: setInterval without corresponding clearInterval will leak memory over time in long-running server processes.
rules.remediation: Store the interval ID and call clearInterval in cleanup/shutdown handlers.

rules.name: Flask Development Server Used in Production
rules.note: Flask's built-in server (app.run()) is single-threaded and not designed for production traffic. It will drop requests under load.
rules.remediation: Use Gunicorn: gunicorn --bind 0.0.0.0:8000 --workers 4 app:app. Or Waitress for Windows.

rules.name: .env File Without .env.example
rules.note: An .env file exists but no .env.example template was detected. New developers won't know which environment variables are required.
rules.remediation: Create .env.example with all required keys (values blanked) and commit it to the repo.

rules.name: Kubernetes Deployment Missing Readiness Probe
rules.note: Pod has no readinessProbe. Kubernetes will route traffic to pods before they're ready, causing 502/503 errors during deployments.
rules.remediation: Add readinessProbe with httpGet on your health endpoint: readinessProbe: { httpGet: { path: /health, port: 8080 }, initialDelaySeconds: 5 }

rules.name: Kubernetes Deployment Missing Liveness Probe
rules.note: Pod has no livenessProbe. If the app deadlocks or hangs, Kubernetes won't restart it — the pod stays up but stops serving.
rules.remediation: Add livenessProbe: { httpGet: { path: /health, port: 8080 }, initialDelaySeconds: 15, periodSeconds: 10 }

rules.name: Kubernetes Pod Without Resource Limits
rules.note: No CPU/memory limits set. Pod can consume unbounded resources, starving other pods and potentially crashing the node.
rules.remediation: Add resources: { requests: { cpu: 100m, memory: 128Mi }, limits: { cpu: 500m, memory: 512Mi } }

rules.name: Tauri DevTools Enabled in Production Window Config
rules.note: tauri.conf.json explicitly enables DevTools ("devtools": true) on a release window. A pirate can open DevTools and mutate Zustand/Redux store state to bypass client-side license, auth, or paywall gates — every Pro feature unlocks instantly with one console line.
rules.remediation: Set "devtools": false in the windows config, and confirm Cargo.toml's tauri dependency does not include the "devtools" feature. Combine with server-side (Rust command) enforcement so a JS-layer bypass yields no Pro data.

rules.name: Tauri Cargo Feature Includes DevTools
rules.note: Cargo.toml opts the tauri crate into the "devtools" feature, which forces DevTools into release builds. Same exposure as DP33 — frontend state mutation bypasses client-side gates.
rules.remediation: Remove "devtools" from the tauri features list. Tauri keeps DevTools in debug builds automatically; do not gate it via a feature for production.

rules.name: Tauri Windows Code Signing Not Configured
rules.note: tauri.conf.json has "certificateThumbprint": null on Windows. Unsigned binaries trigger SmartScreen warnings, look identical to repackaged cracked builds, and let pirates distribute modified versions without OS-level detection.
rules.remediation: Obtain a Windows code-signing certificate (DigiCert, Sectigo, etc.) and set certificateThumbprint to the SHA1 of the certificate. Required before public release.

rules.name: Tauri macOS Code Signing Not Configured
rules.note: tauri.conf.json has "signingIdentity": null on macOS. Unsigned macOS apps trigger Gatekeeper, won't notarize, and let pirates distribute modified versions without OS-level detection.
rules.remediation: Set signingIdentity to your Apple Developer ID Application certificate name (e.g., "Developer ID Application: Your Name (TEAMID)") and configure entitlements + notarization in your release pipeline.

rules.name: Electron Node Integration Enabled in Renderer
rules.note: BrowserWindow is created with nodeIntegration: true. Any XSS in the renderer becomes RCE — attacker JavaScript can require('child_process').exec() with full host privileges. This is the #1 Electron security mistake.
rules.remediation: Set nodeIntegration: false (default in modern Electron) and use a preload script with contextBridge.exposeInMainWorld() to expose only the APIs you need. Never run untrusted content with Node integration on.

rules.name: Electron Context Isolation Disabled
rules.note: BrowserWindow has contextIsolation: false. The renderer and preload script share a global scope, so a prototype pollution or supply-chain XSS in the renderer can rewrite the preload's exposed APIs and escalate to RCE.
rules.remediation: Set contextIsolation: true (default in Electron 12+). Use contextBridge.exposeInMainWorld() in the preload to expose APIs safely.

rules.name: Electron Web Security Disabled
rules.note: BrowserWindow has webSecurity: false, which disables the same-origin policy. Any loaded page can read cross-origin resources, including local files via file:// URLs. Combined with Node integration this is full RCE; alone it's a serious data-exfiltration vector.
rules.remediation: Remove webSecurity: false. It exists solely as a development convenience and has no legitimate use in a release build. Solve your CORS issue another way (proxy, preload IPC, etc.).
